core_behavior: Every request to a protected resource or operation must pass through the access control layer before execution begins.
The system evaluates a triple of (principal, action, resource) and returns a binary decision: PERMIT or DENY.
Authorization decisions are deny-by-default: any principal-action-resource combination not explicitly permitted is denied.
The system supports Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) as the primary model, with Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) extensions for fine-grained policies.
Permissions may be assigned directly to principals or inherited through role membership.
A principal may hold multiple roles simultaneously; permissions are the union of all role permissions unless an explicit deny overrides them.
Explicit DENY rules take precedence over any PERMIT rules at the same or lower specificity level.
Roles are named collections of permissions.
Roles may be hierarchical: a child role inherits all permissions of its parent role(s).
Role inheritance cycles are prohibited and must be detected and rejected at configuration time.
The system must support at minimum the following built-in roles: superadmin, admin, user, guest, and service.
Custom roles may be defined by administrators.
A permission is defined as a tuple: (action, resource_type, resource_scope).
action is a verb such as create, read, update, delete, execute, list, approve, or a wildcard *.
resource_type is a category of resource (e.g., document, user_account, report, api_endpoint).
resource_scope is own (resources owned by the principal), group (resources within the principal's group), or all (any resource of that type).
Wildcard matching is supported: * on action matches all actions; * on resource_type matches all resource types.

Policy evaluation order: (1) Check for an explicit DENY rule matching the request. If found, return DENY immediately. (2) Check for an explicit PERMIT rule matching the request. If found, return PERMIT. (3) If no rule matches, return DENY (default-deny).

Authorization decisions may incorporate contextual attributes including time of day / day of week, IP address or network range of the requestor, device trust level (managed vs. unmanaged), and multi-factor authentication status of the session.
Contextual conditions are expressed as policy predicates attached to permission rules.
The system logs access events.
The system provides administrative interfaces for managing permissions.
inputs: principal_id: string (UUID), required — Unique identifier of the requesting principal.
principal_type: enum (user, service_account, api_key), required — Type of the requesting principal.
action: string, required — The operation being attempted.
resource_type: string, required — Category of the target resource.
resource_id: string, optional — Specific resource instance identifier.
resource_owner_id: string, optional — Owner of the resource, for scope evaluation.
resource_group_id: string, optional — Group the resource belongs to.
context: object, optional — Contextual attributes (IP, time, MFA status, device).
session_id: string, optional — Active session identifier for audit correlation.
Administrative input — Role definition: name, description, parent roles, list of permissions.
outputs: The system returns a binary authorization decision: PERMIT or DENY.
assumptions: The system governs authenticated principals (users, services, or automated agents).
Authorization decisions are enforced at runtime.
failure_modes: Role inheritance cycles are prohibited and must be detected and rejected at configuration time.
edge_cases: If no rule matches, return DENY (default-deny).
Explicit DENY rules take precedence over any PERMIT rules at the same or lower specificity level.
A principal may hold multiple roles simultaneously; permissions are the union of all role permissions unless an explicit deny overrides them.
Wildcard matching is supported: * on action matches all actions; * on resource_type matches all resource types.
